public_boundary: This report exposes claim lineage, source references, contradiction counts, and confidence summaries only. It does not expose hidden chain-of-thought, private substrate state, or Inside Voice internals.
method_summary: Major conclusions were admitted only if supported by evidence graph nodes with source line ranges. Contradictions were preserved as explicit pressure on confidence rather than flattened away.

major_conclusions.conclusion: The Challenger disaster was immediately caused by failure of the right Solid Rocket Motor aft field joint seals, allowing hot combustion gases to escape.
major_conclusions.confidence_basis: Film, telemetry, recovered-wreckage analysis, and the Commission cause chapter converge on the right SRM joint and seal failure.

major_conclusions.conclusion: Engineering contributors included a faulty and poorly understood tang/clevis SRM field-joint seal design, O-ring distress history, temperature-sensitive O-ring resiliency, joint rotation/gap opening, putty pressure-delay behavior, and overreliance on secondary seal assumptions.
major_conclusions.confidence_basis: The exact micro-mechanical contribution of every variable is partly unresolved, but the joint/seal vulnerability, cold sensitivity, and secondary-seal-risk evidence are strong.

major_conclusions.conclusion: Organizational contributors included inadequate anomaly tracking, repeated launch-constraint waivers or omissions, weak safety-program independence, misclassification or misunderstanding of criticality, Marshall information containment, and schedule/operational pressure.
major_conclusions.confidence_basis: Multiple independent chapters trace the same organizational pattern, though schedule pressure is a contributing condition rather than a standalone cause.

major_conclusions.conclusion: The key decision failure was reversing an engineering no-launch recommendation under NASA challenge while failing to preserve and escalate the uncertainty, contrary engineering views, and launch-constraint context to the senior launch decision structure.
major_conclusions.confidence_basis: The no-launch recommendation, management reversal, and incomplete escalation are all directly documented.

major_conclusions.conclusion: The earliest warning signals were design/test indications that the joint seal behavior was not fully understood, followed by flight evidence of O-ring erosion and blow-by beginning early in Shuttle operations and becoming severe and temperature-relevant by STS 51-C and 51-B.
major_conclusions.confidence_basis: The documents strongly show a warning progression; the exact earliest document-level warning depends on whether design documents, test data, or post-flight anomalies define the boundary.

major_conclusions.conclusion: The most important causal pathway was not merely cold weather or a defective part; it was known seal/joint vulnerability plus cold-weather sealing risk, transformed into disaster by normalized anomalies, flawed risk communication, management reversal, and launch approval outside the demonstrated experience base.
major_conclusions.confidence_basis: The pathway integrates the highest-support physical sequence with the highest-impact organizational and decision contradictions.
